questions.question: What is the main concern in CPU scheduling according to the lecture?
questions.explanation: CPU burst distribution is the main concern in CPU scheduling, as it directly affects the scheduling decisions and performance.

questions.question: In which type of scheduling can a process execution be suspended at any point?
questions.explanation: Preemptive scheduling allows the operating system to suspend a running process at any time, typically to allocate the CPU to another process.

questions.question: When a process is in the CPU and execution cannot be suspended until it completes the entire execution, this is called:
questions.explanation: In non-preemptive scheduling, a process runs to completion once it gets the CPU; it cannot be suspended mid-execution.

questions.question: What is the dispatcher?
questions.option_b: B. A module that transfers control of the CPU from one process to another
questions.option_c: C. A type of process queue
questions.explanation: The dispatcher is a module responsible for giving control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.

questions.question: What is dispatch latency?
questions.option_a: A. The time it takes for a process to complete execution
questions.option_b: B. The time a process spends waiting in the ready queue
questions.option_c: C. The time it takes for the dispatcher to stop one process and start another to run
questions.option_d: D. The time between process creation and termination
questions.explanation: Dispatch latency is the time required for the dispatcher to switch from one process to another, including context switch overhead.

questions.question: Which scheduling criterion refers to the number of processes that complete their execution per time unit?
questions.explanation: Throughput measures how many processes are completed per unit time, indicating the system's efficiency.

questions.question: The amount of time it takes from when a request was submitted until the first response is produced is called:
questions.explanation: Response time is the interval from submission of a request until the first response begins, important for interactive systems.

questions.question: In the FCFS example with processes P1 (24ms), P2 (3ms), and P3 (3ms) arriving in the order P1, P2, P3, what is the average waiting time?
questions.explanation: Average waiting time is (0 + 24 + 27) / 3 = 17ms, as P1 waits 0, P2 waits 24, and P3 waits 27ms.

questions.question: What is the convoy effect in FCFS scheduling?
questions.option_a: A. When all processes arrive at the same time
questions.option_b: B. When shorter processes are forced to wait behind longer processes, leading to inefficient execution
questions.option_c: C. When processes are executed in order of priority
questions.option_d: D. When the CPU is idle most of the time
questions.explanation: The convoy effect occurs when short processes wait behind a long process, reducing CPU and device utilization.

questions.question: Which scheduling algorithm is considered optimal for giving the minimum average waiting time?
questions.explanation: SJF is optimal in terms of minimizing average waiting time for a given set of processes, assuming CPU bursts are known.

questions.question: What is the main practical limitation of SJF scheduling?
questions.option_a: A. It causes starvation of long processes
questions.option_b: B. It requires too much memory
questions.option_c: C. It is difficult to know the length of the next/future CPU request
questions.option_d: D. It only works with preemptive scheduling
questions.explanation: In practice, the length of the next CPU burst is hard to predict, making pure SJF infeasible.

questions.question: In the non-preemptive SJF example with processes P1(6), P2(8), P3(7), P4(3) arriving at the same time, what is the average waiting time?
questions.explanation: With order P4, P1, P3, P2, waiting times are 0, 3, 9, 16, averaging (0+3+9+16)/4 = 7ms.

questions.question: Preemptive SJF is also known as:
questions.explanation: Preemptive SJF, where a new shorter job can preempt the current one, is called Shortest-Remaining-Time-First.

questions.question: What problem can occur in priority scheduling where low priority processes may never execute?
questions.explanation: Starvation happens when lower-priority processes are indefinitely delayed due to continuous arrival of higher-priority processes.

questions.question: What is the solution to the starvation problem in priority scheduling?
questions.option_c: C. Aging - where priority increases with time
questions.explanation: Aging gradually increases the priority of waiting processes, ensuring they eventually get CPU time.

questions.question: In priority scheduling, if smaller integers represent higher priority, what priority value would have the highest priority?
questions.explanation: With the convention that smaller integer means higher priority, the smallest value (1) has the highest priority.

questions.question: In the priority scheduling example on Slide 17, what is the waiting time for process P5?
questions.explanation: According to the slide, P5 has a waiting time of 1ms, based on the given calculation.

questions.question: In Round Robin scheduling, what is a time quantum (q)?
questions.option_a: A. The total execution time of a process
questions.option_b: B. The time a process spends waiting in the ready queue
questions.option_c: C. A small unit of CPU time given to each process, typically 10-100 ms
questions.explanation: The time quantum is the fixed slice of CPU time allocated to each process in Round Robin scheduling.

questions.question: In Round Robin scheduling, what happens when the time quantum (q) is very large?
questions.option_a: A. It becomes like SJF scheduling
questions.option_b: B. It becomes like FCFS (FIFO) scheduling
questions.option_c: C. It causes too much overhead
questions.option_d: D. It eliminates context switching completely
questions.explanation: A very large time quantum makes Round Robin effectively FCFS, as each process runs to completion before the next starts.

questions.question: In Multilevel Queue scheduling, what problem can occur with fixed priority scheduling between queues?
questions.explanation: If a higher-priority queue always has processes, lower-priority queues may starve when fixed priorities are used.

questions.question: In the Multilevel Queue example, what percentage of CPU time might be given to the foreground queue using Round Robin?
questions.explanation: An example allocation gives 80% CPU time to the foreground (interactive) queue and 20% to background.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as an evaluation method for CPU scheduling algorithms?
questions.explanation: The lecture lists deterministic evaluation, queueing models, simulations, and implementation; theoretical analysis alone is not a listed method.

questions.question: According to Little's Law (n = λ × W), if on average 7 processes arrive per second and normally 14 processes are in the queue, what is the average wait time per process?
questions.explanation: Using n = λ × W, 14 = 7 × W gives W = 2 seconds, the average waiting time per process.
